name: Decades
description: Key events, figures, and culture across 20th-century decades

questions.q: What was the name of the NASA mission that landed the first humans on the Moon in 1969?

source.notes: July 20, 1969; Neil Armstrong became the first person to walk on the Moon.

questions.q: Who was U.S. President when the stock market crashed in October 1929?

source.notes: Served 1929–1933; succeeded by FDR after a landslide 1932 election.

questions.q: Who wrote the 1925 Jazz Age novel 'The Great Gatsby'?

source.notes: Set on Long Island during the 1920s; tells the tale of Jay Gatsby.

questions.q: In which year did the D-Day Allied landings take place on the beaches of Normandy?

source.notes: June 6, 1944; Operation Overlord.

questions.q: Who was U.S. President for most of World War II, dying in office in April 1945?

source.notes: FDR won four presidential elections; only President to serve more than two terms.

questions.q: Which energetic dance, popular with 1920s 'flappers', gave the Jazz Age its signature moves?

source.notes: Named after Charleston, South Carolina; peaked in popularity around 1925.

questions.q: Charles Lindbergh became famous in 1927 by making the first solo non-stop flight across which body of water?

source.notes: Flew the 'Spirit of St. Louis' from New York to Paris in 33.5 hours.

questions.q: Jesse Owens won four gold medals at which politically charged Olympics?

source.notes: African-American Owens famously embarrassed Hitler's regime by dominating the track events.

questions.q: What was the name of the first artificial satellite launched by the Soviet Union in 1957?

source.notes: Launched October 4, 1957; triggered the Space Race.

questions.q: The famous Woodstock music festival, headlined by Jimi Hendrix and Janis Joplin, took place in which year?

source.notes: August 15–18, 1969, in Bethel, New York.

questions.q: Prohibition-era gangster Al Capone was most closely associated with which U.S. city?

source.notes: Ran the Chicago Outfit from 1925; imprisoned for tax evasion in 1931.

questions.q: Amelia Earhart became famous in the 1930s as a pioneering what?

source.notes: First woman to fly solo across the Atlantic in 1932; disappeared 1937 attempting a round-the-world flight.

questions.q: The October 29, 1929 U.S. stock market crash that triggered the Great Depression is known as what?

source.notes: Preceded by 'Black Thursday' (Oct. 24) and 'Black Monday' (Oct. 28).

questions.q: The Hindenburg, which exploded over New Jersey in 1937, was what kind of aircraft?

source.notes: A hydrogen-filled German passenger airship; 36 of 97 aboard died.

questions.q: Carrie Fisher played which iconic character in the original 'Star Wars' trilogy (1977–1983)?

source.notes: Also reprised the role in the sequel trilogy (2015–2019).

questions.q: Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his 'I Have a Dream' speech in which year?

source.notes: August 28, 1963, at the March on Washington.

questions.q: Rosa Parks's 1955 bus protest touched off the bus boycott in which U.S. city?

source.notes: The Montgomery Bus Boycott lasted 381 days.

questions.q: Which British band, featuring John, Paul, George, and Ringo, dominated the 1960s?
The Cuban Missile Crisis, a nuclear standoff between the U.S. and USSR, occurred in which year?

source.notes: 13-day confrontation in October 1962.

questions.q: Who was the first human to orbit the Earth, doing so in April 1961?

source.notes: Flew aboard Vostok 1 on April 12, 1961.

questions.q: The 1947 Partition of British India created India and which other new country?

source.notes: Bangladesh would later separate from Pakistan in 1971.

questions.q: The 1945–1946 Nuremberg Trials prosecuted which group for war crimes?

source.notes: Held in Nuremberg, Germany; 12 of 24 major defendants received death sentences.
